summary:
Maimonides (Moses ben Maimon, Rambam, 1138–1204 AD) was the preeminent Jewish philosopher, physician, and legal codifier of the medieval period, born in Córdoba in Al-Andalus and later settled in Cairo, whose synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy with Jewish theology profoundly shaped Jewish intellectual tradition and influenced Christian and Islamic philosophy alike. Forced to flee Córdoba when the Almohad dynasty imposed conversion or exile on non-Muslims around 1148, Maimonides' family traveled through North Africa before settling in Fustat (Old Cairo), where he became court physician to the Ayyubid vizier and was recognized as the leader (nagid) of Egyptian Jewry. His prodigious output across law, medicine, philosophy, and biblical commentary earned him the famous epitaph: 'From Moses [of the Torah] to Moses [Maimonides], there was none like Moses.'

Maimonides' intellectual legacy rests on three monumental works. The Mishneh Torah (1180) was an unprecedented systematic codification of all Jewish law — the first attempt to present the entire halakhic tradition in clear, logical Hebrew prose organized by topic rather than following the order of the Talmud, eliminating the need to consult the Talmud directly and provoking fierce controversy from traditionalists who feared it would replace Talmudic study. The Guide for the Perplexed (Moreh Nevukhim, 1190) was his masterwork of philosophy: written for educated Jews confused by the apparent contradictions between Aristotelian philosophy and biblical religion, it argued through sophisticated allegorical interpretation that reason and revelation were ultimately compatible, that anthropomorphic descriptions of God in scripture were metaphorical, and that Aristotle's cosmology could be harmonized with Jewish theology. His medical works (including treatises on asthma, poisons, and regimen of health) were widely translated and used by Christian and Muslim physicians. [[event:3616|Averroes]] and Maimonides, near-contemporaries in Andalusia, represent the parallel Jewish and Muslim engagement with Aristotle that transmitted Greek philosophy to medieval Europe.
